title: Quiz: The SCF Procedure
description: Test your understanding of the self-consistent field algorithm

questions.question: Why is the SCF procedure iterative?

options.text: Because the Fock matrix depends on its own solutions
Because quantum mechanics is inherently iterative

questions.explanation: The Fock matrix F contains Coulomb and Exchange operators that depend on the orbitals (via the density matrix P). Since we're solving for these orbitals, we must iterate until the input and output are consistent.

questions.question: In the Roothaan-Hall equations FC = SCε, why is S present?

options.text: Because the basis functions are not orthonormal

questions.explanation: The overlap matrix S appears because atomic orbital basis functions are not orthonormal—they overlap with each other. If S = I (orthonormal basis), we'd have a standard eigenvalue problem.

questions.question: What does DIIS stand for and what does it do?

options.text: Direct Integration of Iterative Systems - speeds up integrals
Direct Inversion in the Iterative Subspace - accelerates SCF convergence
Density Integrated Iteration Scheme - improves density
Differential Inverse Iteration Solver - solves equations

questions.explanation: DIIS (Direct Inversion in the Iterative Subspace) extrapolates the best Fock matrix from previous iterations by minimizing an error metric. It typically reduces SCF iterations from 30+ to about 10-15.

questions.question: What is the typical scaling of Fock matrix construction?
questions.explanation: Naive Fock matrix construction scales as O(N⁴) because the two-electron contribution requires summing over four indices: Fμν = Σλσ Pλσ[(μν|λσ) - ½(μσ|λν)]. Screening and density fitting can reduce this.

questions.question: The 'core Hamiltonian guess' is generally the best initial guess for SCF.
questions.explanation: False. The core Hamiltonian guess (ignoring e-e repulsion) is simple but often poor, requiring 30+ iterations. SAD (Superposition of Atomic Densities) is typically better, giving convergence in 10-15 iterations.

questions.question: What does level shifting do to help SCF convergence?

options.text: Increases the number of basis functions

questions.explanation: Level shifting adds a constant to the virtual orbital energies during SCF, increasing the HOMO-LUMO gap. This prevents unwanted mixing between occupied and virtual orbitals, stabilizing convergence.

questions.question: In the DIIS error vector e = FPS - SPF, what does convergence (e → 0) imply?
questions.explanation: The DIIS error e = FPS - SPF is related to the commutator [F, P]. At convergence, F and P must commute because they share the same eigenvectors (the converged MOs).

questions.question: Which of the following can cause SCF convergence problems? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: a) Near-degenerate orbitals can swap occupation each iteration. b) Transition metals often have multiple near-degenerate d-orbitals. d) Poor guesses start far from the solution. Larger basis sets (c) don't inherently cause problems, though they may expose other issues.
